> Do you think there is any way gdbgui could auto-detect that it is dealing with a Rust program to avoid the 'main' warning?

Yes, it could run the command `show language` then parse its output (I don't know of a gdb machine interface command). And then I assume if the language is rust or go (which also has this issue), then you think it would be helpful if gdbgui would print a warning saying `breakpoint will not be added because language is rust`. LMK if that sounds good and I can put it on my TODO list.

More details on why this happens: gdbgui runs the gdb command `-break-insert main`, and then gets a breakpoint from gdb. The breakpoint has a field called `fullfile` which is usually a path to a file, but in this case gdb cannot find the function and/or file and it just puts a breakpoint at an address. So the `fullfile` file is just called main, and then gdbgui tries to open it and it fails.

Ok, I was assuming that having an --only parameter implied that the search would become non-recursive (and therefore was an alternative suggestion to have both --directories (or any name) and --recursive).

This is a matter of preference indeed. Personally, I find --directories more explicit, but maybe the opinion of a native English speaker would be more useful.

By the way, I realize there is already a `-s SRC, --src SRC` option, so the simplest would be to just extend it to accept a list : `-s SRC [SRC ...], --src SRC [SRC ...]`. Then either add a `--non-recursive` flag, or change the default behavior and add a `-r, --recursive` flag (still my preferred way, according to my experience of trying to avoid surprises as much as possible, with higher priority compared to trying to automate everything)

I agree. But a huge problem with GTF/GFF files in the wild is that they don't follow the spec, and one of my goals with gffutils is to still support those crazy derivatives. I could have sworn I had played with this in the past, and after a quick check looks like I did [here](https://github.com/daler/gffutils/blob/master/gffutils/parser.py#L275):

```python
        # TODO: urllib.unquote breaks round trip invariance for "hybrid1.gff3"
        # test file.  This is because the "Note" field has %xx escape chars,
        # but "Dbxref" has ":" which, if everything were consistent, should
        # have also been escaped.
        #
        # (By the way, GFF3 spec says only literal use of \t, \n, \r, %, and
        # control characters should be encoded)
        #
        # Solution 1: don't unquote
        # Solution 2: store, along with each attribute, whether or not it
        #             should be quoted later upon reconstruction
        # Solution 3: don't care about invariance
       # unquoted = [urllib.unquote(v) for v in vals]
```

So it looks like I went with the don't-unquote strategy. However I don't think it's appropriate to avoid useful functionality solely based on corner cases. How about adding a config option `constants.ignore_url_escape_characters` which is by default False to support unquoting before storing in the database, but can be set to True in cases like the above problematic example?

In such corner cases, it would be important to set it to True when writing out in order to get round trip invariance. Maybe the value of `ignore_url_escape_characters` could be stored in the database's `meta` table for persistence. Then in a later session, if that `meta` value is not consistent with the current `ignore_url_escape_characters`, a warning/reminder can be printed when a connection to the db is made.

Frankly I have not done that myself, that requires having a worth-while package and I do not have one :)

According to the documents [here](https://wiki.python.org/moin/CheeseShopTutorial) once initial channels are set up and ready, the package owner can do e.g. `python setup.py sdist upload` for any new release.
After that users can use `pip install dlib`. For users on Windows and OSX having a wheel channel (`bdist_wheel`) helps them not needing any compilation from sources (setting up `cmake`, ...), for Linux users `sdist` channel is fine.

So I've been messing around with this a bit. I updated to the latest ActiveTcl on my machine as, which didn't fix anything (also tried reinstalling python as well). After some scavenging I *think* I found the source of the problem. From this [matplotlib doc](https://matplotlib.org/tutorials/introductory/usage.html#what-is-a-backend) on backends:

> To make things a little more customizable for graphical user interfaces, matplotlib separates the concept of the renderer (the thing that actually does the drawing) from the canvas (the place where the drawing goes). The canonical renderer for user interfaces is Agg which uses the Anti-Grain Geometry C++ library to make a raster (pixel) image of the figure. All of the user interfaces except macosx can be used with agg rendering, e.g., WXAgg, GTKAgg, QT4Agg, QT5Agg, TkAgg.

With this and a little more messing around, I ended up modifying the init file to overwrite the matplotlib backend to 'TkAgg' and it works perfectly now. I'd be interested to see if this fixes the problem on all OSXs and doesn't introduce others in other operating systems. I'm a bit of a novice, but I've considered submitting a PR to solve this issue. But it's a very small fix and again, I'm not sure if it extends generally. Thoughts? Any other OSX users coming across this problem?

Telnet support is fine. This looks like you are connecting via terminal server (GNS3 console, however). In other words, the above behavior is not the normal behavior for a Cisco router and telnet (which would prompt for a username/password).

You probably want to use the following device_type instead:  `generic_termserver_telnet`

You can then use `read_channel` and `write_channel` to read all of the extra messages on the screen and to hit enter (`\n`).

After you are fully logged in then you can use a redispatch to `cisco_ios_telnet`.

See this for more details:

https://github.com/ktbyers/netmiko/blob/develop/COMMON_ISSUES.md#does-netmiko-support-connecting-via-a-terminal-server

Oh. I think I get it. The `@synchronized` function, upon completion, does the *equivalent* of doing `.wait()` *inside* it.

It's definitely possible! The concurrent function already acts as sort of a work queue, each time it's called it's sort of like an item being added to the work queue. This queue does get periodically cleared when a synchronization event occurs, but as long as there is no synchronization every new call to concurrent functions gets sort of pushed into the work queue even after the work has been started.

      s = rom.String()

    self.assertEqual(x.s, None)
    x.s = "string"

    x.s = None
    self.assertIsNone(x.s)
    x.s = None # <-- AttributeError

A question that seems to be lingering in my mind for my projects, pydicom, and other libraries of this type is, "Do we really need it though?"

I see one difference between the environments described there and those of pydicom and other libraries, and that is the API aspect. When changes are constantly being worked on all day long, and it's internal, CI makes a whole lotta sense. For web development, this makes sense. For a library like pydicom, where backwards-incompatible changes might be introduced, this seems to make much less sense. E.g. what if someone finds a severe bug in pydicom <1.0; if there was only a master branch that was constantly merged to, that person just got left behind, and must upgrade to the most stable release no matter what.

I haven't looked through in great detail, but in scanning the code again, I'm now wondering about my comment suggesting doing this at the `read_dataset` level.  For example, since `correct_ambigrous_vr` walks through the dataset and converts any sequence items, then this means the same datasets will be corrected multiple times (well, twice anyway, perhaps more if further sequence nesting occurs) -- once when each inner dataset is read, and again when the dataset containing the sequence recurses into them.  Pydicom has generally taken the philosophy of converting things only as needed, because some use cases require maximum speed for very minor specific changes to files.

Did we ever discuss anywhere doing the conversion at the `Dataset.__getitem__` level?  I haven't had time to think it through, but it seems similar to the conversion of data elements from "raw"; in fact, it would happen after that conversion, and should only happen once.  Since it is a method of `Dataset`, there is access to the other data elements needed to disambiguate the VR.

After a bit of debugging, I found a workaround, but no real fix yet...

```diff
diff --git a/travispy/_helpers.py b/travispy/_helpers.py
index 348d816..f54636b 100644
--- a/travispy/_helpers.py
+++ b/travispy/_helpers.py
@@ -16,7 +16,10 @@ def get_response_contents(response):
     '''
     status_code = response.status_code
     try:
-        contents = response.json()
+        if 'json' in response.headers['content-type']:
+            contents = response.json()
+        else:
+            contents = {'log': {'type': 'Log', 'body': response.text}}
     except:
         error = response.text.strip()
         if not error:
```

The problem is that the log is provided as a value of type `text/plain`, while `get_response_contents` assumes it's always dealing with a value of type `application/json`.

The return value of `get_response_contents` should be a `dict` value though (usually a parsed JSON value), that's assumed by the rest of the code.

A dirty workaround is to manually construct the expected `dict` value for logs when the response value type is not `application/json`, see patch above.

There must be a cleaner way to fix this though, at least only do this when the response is actually for a log, and not for something else that may be of type `text/plain`...

@Osiris1975 You may need to pass the pagination object's items to the table class, then pass the pagination object and the table to your template. It shouldn't upset anything with page navigation.

Eg:
```python
pagination = submissions.paginate(page=page, per_page=ROWS_PER_PAGE)
table = SubTable(pagination.items)
return render_template(
    'index.html',
    submissions_table=table,
    pagination=pagination)
````

Then you have access to things like the current page, and has_next/has_prev etc in your template.

(Relatedly, I would be concerned based on your example above about sometimes passing `submissions=[[Table instance]]` and sometimes passing `submissions=[[list of models]]` to your `index.html` template. Much nicer to be consistent (up to duck-typing) in what you're passing.)

As for your query about paginated tables, because you can do the code as above, I have no intention of adding anything for this. Also, because `flask_table` is just about taking an iterable of some objects and turning them into an html table containing exactly those objects. No filtering, no sorting, no slicing, etc. All of that logic belongs in your view, not in the table.

Hi there!

I think i don't have all the information needed, (what metakernel you are using to load the
data, and how you do load it), but I would guess that you are not loading the DSK through
the spice.furnsh call, and that's why the spice.illumf fails.

From what I see, you have loaded the DSK using spice.dasopr (that returns the handle you
are using for spice.dskx02). But then spice.illumf fails, which means that the data is
not loaded in the kernel pool. In order to load the data in the kernel pool, you need
to use spice.furnsh( DSK ) or spice.furnsh( meta ), and DSK provided within the meta
kernel file.

If that is not the case, please post the list of kernels you are using (or the meta-kernel)
and a snapshot of the code from the point where you load the kernels until the
illu = spice.illumf call.

I hope this helps.
Best regards,

What do you think about the latest changes I did in the PR? It's not breaking any functionality which was there before, it just let's the user decide if he wants to use the `blocking=0` version of pychromecast which would require to call `run_once()` by main loop.
If no `blocking` argument is added the functionality is exactly the same as it was before. So the user can decide if he want's to use
- blocking version: Mainly for scripts or other applications which integrate pychromecast in a separate process/thread
- non-blocking version: Users who have already their own main loop and don't want to have blocking functions (or new processes/threads to make them non-blocking) in place.

Yes, I agree that it will be different. However, it will only differ in `httpretty=0.8.10` which is not the dependency of `smart_open`. It will only differ in a dependency that is never needed in production! Why do you care about if there is an additional library installed on the user's computer, if it is never imported and never used?

Before reading the rest of my discussion, let me stress that my only intention is to help. To help me fix the issue and to help other who might encounter it. I am not trying to compromise test and stability. I wouldn't be proposing this otherwise. If you convince me that doing this is harmful I will stop and not bring it up again.

Let me try to convince you one more time. Here are my reasons:

- If you want the environment in the wild to be the same as on Travis (which we all strive for), why is it ok to exclude `mock moto responses` from `setup.py`?

- Since `mock moto responses` are not in `setup.py` they will never be installed when installing `smart_open` through pip. There will always be at least this difference between Travis and the wild. If so, why do we care whether `httpretty` is on the system and which version? It will never be used anyway. When pip installing you cannot run test, since `mock moto responses` are missing, so why do we care that there is a right version of `httpretty`? What difference does it make?

- If `httpretty` is only needed by `moto` and not by `smart_open`, why is then `moto` installed only for Travis (through `.travis`) and `httpretty` through `setup.py`? Shouldn't they both be either in `.travis` or in `setup.py`?

- Imagine that `moto` fixes this tomorrow and decides that it needs a `0.8.14` version of `httpretty`. What would happen? They would issue a new release, the next time your Travis run it would take this new version of `moto` and break due to a version conflict. Then you would have to fix it here and also make a new release. That is an unnecessary duplication. My reasoning is the following. We should not even think about `httpretty`. All we really need is a working version of `moto`. Do you agree? If so, we should let the authors of `moto` decide which versions they want — as they are currently doing by pinning the version. If the current version of `moto` is working for us, what do we gain by pining `httpretty`? On Travis we get `0.8.10` either way, with pip it currently breaks if LC_ALL=C, but could work if the pin is removed. Further, if we were to pin anything, which I oppose if not really required, the only thing we could pin is the `moto` itself, not its dependencies. We require `moto`, we do not require `httpretty`, therefore only `moto` should be among our dependencies.

- Look at #67. There you removed `requests` from `setup.py`. Why can't we do the same for `httpretty`? In what sense would we compromise test with removing the pin? They would run on exactly the same environment and the pass/fail decisions would not be affected. All we would be changing is what gets installed when we use pip.

Hope that you understand my concerns. Please, let me know what you think about this.

learn_bpe terminates under two conditions:
 - you have reached the defined number of merge operations.
 - there is no pair of subword units that occurs more than once.
if you try to learn BPE operations on a small corpus, you might encounter the second condition. Depending on your use case, this might be fine, but I would usually recommend to use a large corpus to learn BPE operations.

The behavior of indexing a structured NumPy array with a list containing multiple field names has changed: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/user/basics.rec.html#accessing-multiple-fields

I don't know which version introduced the change, but the effect is that whereas `element.data[['red', 'green', 'blue']].dtype` used to be 

    dtype([('red', 'u1'), ('green', 'u1'), ('blue', 'u1')])

it is now

    dtype({'names':['red','green','blue'], 'formats':['u1','u1','u1'], 'offsets':[12,13,14], 'itemsize':23})

So `element.data[['red', 'green', 'blue']].view(np.uint8)` will now see all 23 bytes of each record, even the ones that aren't accessible via the dtype's fields.

A simple but inefficient alternative is

    np.asarray(element.data[['red', 'green', 'blue']].tolist(), np.uint8)

Another way is to pre-allocate an array of the correct shape and type using `np.empty` and then copy each record over.

def __init__(_self,i:_type_i):

>>> s = Server(None)

Yeah, I've seen similar problems with waffle and DRF. DRF has its own `Request` class and doesn't have a public API to accessing the original Django/wsgi request object, which is really unfortunate. (Last I checked, it was available at `request._request`, which is private and undocumented, afaict.) I really don't want to modify the core code of Django libraries to support DRF's weirdness, especially when it depends on private APIs.

In this case, I think @adamchainz is right that moving `@ratelimit` to the top of the chain would fix it, which should be OK since you're using IP-based ratelimiting. But if you wanted to use `user_or_ip` and were using DRF's token authentication, I think you'd need to write a custom decorator that used the `is_ratelimited` function directly.

I don't want to modify the core code, but I'd be open to adding a `ratelimit.ext.drf` module that had a DRF-compatible decorator and/or `APIView` mixin or similar.

Hi ramineniraviteja.

this happened to me as well. I think that is because the resolution of the image you use to compare with is low. think about this, we use pic A, whose resolution is low, as sample person. and we aim to find the other pic B of same face in your database.  However, the resolution of pics in your huge database is pretty high, comparing to the pic A. the accuracy cannot be high. 

the machine would generate an embedding(128 measurements) for each face, which is actually used for comparing.  they comes from some features of the face, such as the "proportion of the lengths of eyes and mouth...etc. however if the resolution is low, one pixel offset could cause the "proportions" changes a lot, though the code will still  generate an embedding. However the result is not that confident. then it lack the ability to distinguish different people, because it cannot get the accurate features(with embedding) of each face.

this issue happens a lot when the sample pic and target pic's resolution do not match.  it is still a way off. 
if you do some basic search on data form of image, you can tell the difference between "pixel image" which get blurred after being zoomed in; and "vector image", which is still clear while zooming in and out. 
pixels are the most superficial way to define a picture, and the manage pixels are the hardest.  
maybe in the future, a new data form of picture could be invented, which is specific for store human face, which contains only the proportions and other feature of the face instead of pixels.  or, more generally, you can imagine there would be a new data form contains some features can be understood by an "image compiler", which is actually a learning machine.

I take it you've never connected to an outdated LDAP server behind a slow

I don't really want to break people's codebases with an overly-strict
setting. Any timeout is better than no timeout. Failing after 2 seconds
when the LDAP server was going to take 3 seconds to respond due to an
overloaded TCP stack will be seen as a bug.

On 29 September 2017 at 16:42, Alizain Feerasta <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> An LDAP server that takes 10 seconds to respond to a particular query is
> perhaps within a couple std deviations of the average, but a TCP connection
> that is taking more than 10 seconds to establish itself is probably simply
> hung.
>
> I'm not a network expert, but a quick google search reveals the following
> numbers:
>
> Local (~15ms RTT) - 22.5ms
> Trans-continental (~80ms RTT) - 120ms
> Inter-continental (~150ms RTT) -  225ms
> Satellite (~500ms RTT) - 750ms
>
> On a cursory glance, the following paper
> <https://people.cs.umass.edu/%7Ephillipa/papers/SPECTS.pdf> backs up the
> relative magnitudes.
>
> So I'm completely comfortable in saying that a CONNECT_TIMEOUT of 1 or 2
> seconds will account for 99.99% of all TCP connections established between
> the web and ldap servers.
>
> However, for the RECEIVE_TIMEOUT, as mentioned above, I can totally
> imagine a somewhat larger timeout. Even then, I wouldn't set it higher than
> 10 seconds, because it's on the critical path for login. During this time,
> the user will simply see a loading indicator, and will probably try and
> click "Submit" again. If the ldap server can't respond to a particular
> query within 10 seconds, than I would venture a guess that the ldap server
> is overloaded, and failing quickly is better.

It is the view decorator functions (`@jwt_required`, `@jwt_optional`, etc) that look for the token, do the verifications, perform all the callbacks, and store the token in the flask request context.

Some letters has different transliteration based on their position. This is an example for cyrillic letter "Я":

 * If it is the first letter in the word: **Я**готин -> **Ya**hotyn
 * If it is *not* the first letter in the word: Феодосі**я** -> Feodosi**ia**

Sequence of letters **зг** has next transliteration: **zgh**. So, it should be processed with more high priority than previous specific.

All this mean that we should use regular expressions.

If there was a way of saying "restore DB state such that it will pass all future migrations", then absolutely. But I am not convinced that such a thing exists. And we're using `TransactionTestCase` here, so the DB can't help us with a rollback. Only you with your project know how the data needs to look to pass the future migrations.

As for debugging, it might help if tearDown did a try-except around the `call_command` so it could re-raise and explain saying "failed to restore state in tearDown". Though in py2 without `raise from` this would lose information which is annoying, but probably better than the current situation.

> Good tests should revert the state of any resources they use so I am sure rollbacking automatically the test data should be a good thing. Don't you think?

Absolutely. But we're already through the proverbial looking-glass here. MigrationTest is really abusing Django's TestCase. If we really want guaranteed restoration, then the safest way of getting the db back on track would be to drop it and rebuild which I would love, but then the tests would take a stupidly long time.

Relatedly, [Django's docs mention something similar in the warning in this section](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/testing/tools/#transactiontestcase) and suggests using `serialized_rollback = True`. Not sure if this would help or not. Might be worth trying, or investigating what it does. It also mentions different backends - what DB engine are you using?

So I think the options are:
1. As now but try-except to explain the tearDown failure, and leave the restoration up to the developer.
2. A class-level boolean called something like `flush_db_after_hard_fail` that would basically enable my `call_command('flush')` snippet from before, defaulting to `False`.
3. As above but defaulting to `True`, though this changes existing behaviour and may introduce the problem you found for others.
4. Something else, maybe?

@Fokko I had first changed the failed test (`test_cli_connections_list `) to below:
```python
self.assertIn(['bigquery_default', 'google_cloud_platform'], conns)
```
The CI failed this for `backed_sqlite` & `backend_postgres`, however it passed for the rest which you can see [here](https://travis-ci.org/apache/incubator-airflow/builds/345754806). 

This was because of the way the test (`test_cli_connections_list`) is written. The test grabs the content from the command line and uses regex to extract the required information.
```python
    def test_cli_connections_list(self):
        with mock.patch('sys.stdout',
                        new_callable=six.StringIO) as mock_stdout:
            cli.connections(self.parser.parse_args(['connections', '--list']))
            stdout = mock_stdout.getvalue()
        conns = [[x.strip("'") for x in re.findall("'\w+'", line)[:2]]
                 for ii, line in enumerate(stdout.split('\n'))
                 if ii % 2 == 1]
        conns = [conn for conn in conns if len(conn) > 0]
```

Now the command line strips longer names and presents it as `...`, so instead of `google_cloud_platform` it may show `google...platform...` and instead of `druid_ingest_default` it would end of showing `druid_..._default` (Screenshot 2). Hence the regex won't be able to match it when the space in cli is low and it displays `...` instead of correct names.

**Screenshot 1:** 
Travis CI report showing that because of the dots, the connection list obtained using regex only contain `[u'bigquery_default']` instead of `[u'bigquery_default', u'google_cloud_platform']` and similar with `druid`
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8811558/36635866-694f20dc-19e2-11e8-9646-5bee210cec4d.png)

**Screenshot 2:** 
The screengrab obtained from command line when the connections are listed. You can check using `airflow connections -l`
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8811558/36636001-a4ef3bbc-19e3-11e8-9eff-68d4ec894c64.png)

Hence, I have removed the below line from the test.

```python
self.assertIn(['bigquery_default', 'google_cloud_platform'], conns)
```

@pbauer I think the `buildout` script is replaced because it's in the eggs list of `py3.cfg`. 
The first version of the script you show above is installed by `pip install zc.buildout`. The second version is installed by the buildout configuration itself.

Remove `zc.buildout` from the list of eggs:

```
diff --git a/py3.cfg b/py3.cfg
index 3a052f3..a3a3705 100644
--- a/py3.cfg
+++ b/py3.cfg
@@ -73,7 +73,6 @@ eggs +=
     pdbpp
 #    Products.PDBDebugMode
     plone.reload
-    zc.buildout
     waitress
```

and rerun the buildout. Note that it will maybe remove the `buildout` script the first time after removing it from the eggs list because `bin/buildout` is referenced in your `.installed.cfg`. If that happens, `pip uninstall zc.buildout` and then `pip install zc.buildout` again. After that, running `buildout -c py3.cfg` will not change your `bin/buildout` script anymore.

Update, I think I fixed it for the most part.  I had needed to modify the jupyter_client.session to use eventloop sockets, and it took some tinkering to get things right.  

Essentially they were using zmq.NO_BLOCK by default and catching EAGIN's, but when used on a eventloop socket, that overwhelms the whole eventloop with the phony return value (because it is returning instantly).

My first solution was to take out that whole section of code and replace it with a default socket.recv_multipart call and it worked, most of the time, except when EAGIN's were sent.

Therefore the end solution was to use default options on the recv_multipart, but still try-except for EAGIN's.  This seems to work much better (can't say perfectly yet, not enough testing).

When I've seen weird issues with ipc, it's sometimes been because two processes accidentally 'bind' to the same ipc interface, which doesn't work but won't raise errors. I have also seen issues with unusual bind/connect relationships such as SUB sockets binding. One thing to check is switching transport (try tcp, etc.) and see if things change.
Thank you for this bug fix and your work in general.

@autrilla 

Thank you for the feedback. We truly appreciate any feedback we can get on the project.

It is true that the Boto3 and the CLI share similar methods. This is because both of these projects are separate higher level layers to a lower level library that we maintain: [botocore](https://github.com/boto/botocore>). Botocore is lower level in the sense that it methods are a near direct mapping to the various AWS API's . There are very few customizations added to these methods, as they are added in the higher level libraries/tools such as Boto3 and the CLI.

I do have one question. Have you had a chance to look at the resource interface of boto3?

So boto3 has two different interfaces to interact with AWS's APIs. There is the client interface that you mentioned. The client interface is pretty much the same interface used by botocore, where the methods are nearly direct mappings to the APIs. The main purpose of this interface is to allow users to make more fine-tuned requests to the APIs (i.e. you know exactly what parameters you want to a send in a request and you just want a dictionary back representing what was in the response).

The other interface, which sounds like the one you want is, the [resource interface](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/guide/resources.html). The purpose of this interface is to make interaction more pythonic and object oriented when working with AWS. When you use resources, you are working with auto-generated classes that live onto of the client interface.

To hit some of the points you mentioned, the resource classes have getters (they are labeled as identifiers and attributes) that will actually make the underlying call for you to retrieve the necessary data.  Resources also can create and return other resources to use from a method call, instead of just a dictionary. So for example an s3 [Object](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#object) resource object has the method [initiate_multipart_upload](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#s3.Object.initiate_multipart_upload) that will return to you a s3 [MultipartUpload](https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#s3.MultipartUpload) resource object to work with a multipart upload. Then on top of these resources we have customizations that are built on top of AWS APIs that requires stringing together possiblly multiple API calls. For example, we recently added an ``upload_file`` and ``download_file`` method (note that this is only available from the client for now): https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/customizations/s3.html

Please let us know if you have any follow up questions/comments/feedback/feature requests.  We love getting feedback on how to improve this project.

@trenton42 This is an expected behavior of MongoDB. If you don't explicitly set limit, txmongo will send None for n_to_return and MongoDB will return up to 101 docs in the first batch and up to 16Mb in each following. This is documented here: https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/tutorial/iterate-a-cursor/#cursor-batches (in Tutorials somewhy).

We are using n_to_return only to specify maximum number of documents we want to get and only if limit is specified. This is not the same as batchSize.

> I think a name that conveys their functional difference would be better than something like "old_temporal_memory" (implies outdated or worse) or similar.

The problem is finding which name better describes such TP/TP10x2 _functional difference_.. Anyway, maybe such _functional difference_ could be simply moved into new temporal memory files/classes (not preserved as an old version) as @subutai suggest here:

>I suggest we just focus on creating a C++ version of temporal_memory.py, ensure it works really well and fast, and move everything to it. Then we can just delete Cells4, TP.py, and TP10X2.py This would remove all the naming confusion.
https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/351#issuecomment-75829073

In either case, if even so we reach the consensus that it is better we keep such functional difference in separate files, then I would suggest we use omnibus/generic names like `SpatialProcessor` and `TemporalProcessor` which could atenuate this and future confusions. I.e. not matter if a TP has pooling, memory sequence, whatever, it still would be a TemporalProcessor. To distinguish them, we could use (for example):
- SP => `spatial_processor_v1.py / SpatialProcessorV1`
- TP => `temporal_processor_v1.py / TemporalProcessorV1`
- TP10x2 => `fast_temporal_processor_v1.py / FastTemporalProcessorV1`
- TemporalMemory => `temporal_processor_v2.py / TemporalProcessorV2`
- FastTemporalMemory => `fast_temporal_processor_v2.py / FastTemporalProcessorV2`

(..we could use other convention than `V1` and `V2` to distinguish versions)

In summary, we could find what is the `TP/TP10x2` functional difference, if such functional difference can be moved into new (pure python and c++) `temporal_memory` implementations or then keep it in separated files as is currently (but using generic names with versioning).

I'm working on [removing webuser](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3267) module and I found the following situation:

In webuser, exists a function [nickname_valid_p(nickname)](https://github.com/hachreak/invenio/blob/master/invenio/legacy/webuser.py#L378) to check nickname.
The function **doesn't** consider valid:
 - empty nickname
 - with space before and/or after
 - if it contains `@`

But, in [user registration](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/c7c9b3e68c7d577e687763ac6b4a1e9ba564d5d3/invenio/legacy/webuser.py#L427) the check is bypassed for empty nickname (that, in the end, it's considered a valid nickname).

But, actually, in the `User` model there are no call to these check functions.

Yesterday, I asked to @tiborsimko and @jirikuncar about what we can consider "valid" nickname and they showed me 2 different, but equal "valid", visions about the problem.
So, I'm really interested in this conversation, because I'm moving and refactoring the validation of the nickname [from webuser to User](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3267/files#diff-10f346f902fdab6c4364e5e065da52c2R101).

Here's the concept I'm going for: 

![screenshot from 2018-01-05 15-40-27](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/409011/34629711-cf6fca14-f22e-11e7-9042-ae0e186d2bd2.png)

So I jumped down the rabbit hole - it's an issue with zipfile.ZipFile() guessing the wrong encoding for the extracted filenames. We'll see how GitHub handles the below output, hopefully it's legible:

```
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:test netspi$ unzip unicode.ipa
Archive:  unicode.ipa
<snip>
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:test netspi$ ls
Payload
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:test netspi$ cd Payload/
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:Payload netspi$ ls
Test®†µ˚¬.app # <- Correct name of app bundle, as set in Xcode.
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:test netspi$
NetSPIs-MacBook-Pro:test netspi$ python3
Python 3.6.2 (v3.6.2:5fd33b5926, Jul 16 2017, 20:11:06) 
[GCC 4.2.1 (Apple Inc. build 5666) (dot 3)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import zipfile
>>> file = zipfile.ZipFile('/Users/netspi/Desktop/test/unicode.ipa','r')
>>> file
<zipfile.ZipFile filename='/Users/netspi/Desktop/test/unicode.ipa' mode='r'>
>>> file.namelist()[1]
'Payload/Test┬«ΓÇá┬╡╦Ü┬¼.app/' # <- WRONG representation of filename
>>> file.namelist()[1].encode('cp437') # <- The deep corners of the internet suggested code page 437 is commonly used as a default encoding with the zip format
b'Payload/Test\xc2\xae\xe2\x80\xa0\xc2\xb5\xcb\x9a\xc2\xac.app/' #
>>> file.namelist()[1].encode('cp437').decode('utf-8')
'Payload/Test®†µ˚¬.app/' # <- Sure enough, reversing the cp437 decoding and re-decoding as UTF8 fixes the problem.
```

So! I found a few python issue tickets (such as https://bugs.python.org/issue28080), but it doesn't look like there's a simple way to programmatically guess the encoding of the zip file.

The problem stems from the fact that we use 
```
self.app_binary = os.path.join(self.app_folder, info_plist['CFBundleExecutable'])
```
which gets the properly-encoded name from the plist file, regardless of how the actual filename is decoded from the zip file. If the filename decodes properly from the zip file, this will work. If it doesn't, the binary referenced by the above won't be present in the unzipped archive. Additionally, re-packaging an IPA with incorrectly encoded filenames will cause a similar mismatch when a user tries to install or run a modified IPA.

I'm not sure of the best way to proceed here. Compare the filename in the plist with the extracted filename, perhaps, and use that as a way to determine valid encodings?

Suggestion: We add two optional arguments `node_to_sparql` and `node_from_result` to SPARQLStore's constructor. Both take a function which takes a node and returns a node. All nodes are converted using these functions, not only BNodes. We then predefine two sets of functions: One that just handles skolemization using well-known IRIs, and one that uses `<bnode:>`-URIs. By default, we use a function that raises an exception when a blank node is encountered for `node_to_sparql`, and the identity for `node_from_result`.

The argument `bNodeAsURI` currently does not do anything. And it seems that it never did. It may be that SPARQLWrapper once interpreted this option, but the current version doesn't seem to. So, I would just document it as ignored and deprecated, while keeping it as optional agument to the constructor.

Right.  The comment above the failing line claims that "python's uniform is perfect since the upper limit is not inclusive".  But rand() isn't from python, it's from C.  And the C standard says rand() is inclusive, 0<=rand()<=RAND_MAX.

I think the question is whether the intent was to have (RAND_MAX-1) as the denominator instead of RAND_MAX. That comment makes me think python's random.random() used to be used here.

Secondary questions would be (a) why the C PRNG is being used instead of the python PRNG, since C rand() implementations are usually poor, and (b) why a binomial distribution is being used instead of uniform.

I thought I'd drop an alternate idea, which is to create a new argument name (e.g. `--qualifier` or `--facet`...) so that we don't overload the `--platform` argument.

`--platform foo --platform bar` would be equivalent to `--qualifier foo-any-any-any --qualifier bar-any-any`. `--platform` and `--qualifier` could potentially be mutually exclusive, but not necessarily.

This approach has the disadvantage of creating a new option, but on the other hand, does not overload the `--platform` argument and does not break as many ties with `pip` and hence might be more future-proof.

This is a regression, because we added explicit checks for some unsupported cases that were not checked earlier. But it is a small one that you can fix yourself:

What is the value of RestApiId in the Events structure? The only supported value is `{"Ref": "LogicalId"}` or `"LogicalId"`

### Supported values for RestApiId

**LogicalId of AWS::Serverless::Api resource**
```
Events:
  MyApi:
    Type: Api
     Properties: 
       Path: /foo
       Method: GET
      # Direct value of the logical ID
       RestApiId: MyServerlessApi
```

**"Ref" of LogicalId of AWS::Serverless::Api resource**
```
Events:
  MyApi:
    Type: Api
     Properties: 
       Path: /foo
       Method: GET
      # Ref of the logical ID
       RestApiId: {"Ref": "MyServerlessApi"}
```

### Unsupported

**Any other intrinsic functions**
```
Events:
  MyApi:
    Type: Api
     Properties: 
       Path: /foo
       Method: GET
      # Fn::Sub of the logical ID
       RestApiId: {
          "Fn::Sub": "${MyServerlessApi}"
       }
```

If you happened to use other intrinsic functions, you should migrate to one of the supported structures. There should be absolutely no change in end result for you.

@rtibbles can you recall if this is regarding a .pex built locally or from Buildkite?

I concur from reading the code that this happens because a `kolibri/VERSION` file isn't updated during building of a .pex. This is the only explanation I see from the current version mechanism.

While developing, static data should be served through the devserver, and this shouldn't be a problem.

The only reasonable alternative to **not trusting the version file**, is to run `collectstatic` every time kolibri is started. I recommend that we accept this until the get_version PR #1498 is merged, and then I'll add a revert of this commit in the PR, so it's rolled back once we merge that PR.

One problem, I've found is in Buildkite where the .pex file is being build on top of results from other processes. This is the Make target:

```
pex:
        ls dist/*.whl | while read whlfile; do pex $$whlfile --disable-cache -o dist/kolibri-`unzip -p $$whlfile kolibri/VERSION`.pex -m kolibri --python-shebang=/usr/bin/python$
```

As you can see, it doesn't depend on anything else. If we made it depend on at least writing a new version file, we could avoid building .pex files locally without having triggered a new version file.

I would stay away with something like `header-payload-digests` because we are thinking about it in a more general terms so that the same field can be used in other replay systems such as OWB or PyWB where the field would hold reference to the corresponding WARC file with offset and length like `urn:warcs/{offset}/{length}/{warc_file_name_or_path}/`. In fact the upcoming model of IPWB is planned to not have references to the header and payload, but a single standard `ipfs:` URI reference to a memento node that will internally point to all the related pieces using IPLD.

Note that we are trying to optimize asynchronous requests to IPFS, not the Web. Thanks for your input, @funkyfuture, and as @ibnesayeed said, we will look into using that library as applicable when we can get the rest of the code ported to Py3.

> Will application containers all include a self-signed cert and Nginx? Or just those that have opted into it? This depends on the availability of relevant information (environment variable) at build time.

See #4569 for a clear understanding of how this works:

> The builder produces images that are devoid of "last mile" configs. The builder triggers a release by calling a hook in the controller, and the controller does bake configs into an image during the release process.

> The process looks something like this:

> build image + config == release image

If an HTTPS application endpoint is enabled or disabled by an environment variable set through `deis config:set`, that config gets baked into a new release image, but the builder is not at all involved in that process.

This means when the builder produces a "build image" that is absent all that config, it _must_ include a self-signed cert and Nginx at that time because it is unknown whether or not they will be needed down the road.

At runtime, however, we _can_ eliminate some overhead by not starting Nginx when the app starts if the configuration indicates it's not needed.

List of changes for my proposed approach seems to be shaping up as follows:

* Modify slug-builder to generate self-signed cert
* Modify slug-runner Dockerfile to expose secure port
* Modify slug-runner to include Nginx, including some static configuration
* Modify slug-runner so app start script also starts Nginx (or doesn't if not needed)
* Modify router confd template for router config to use HTTPS for back-end proxy target as appropriate

From what I've been hearing on these issues, we may want to stick with documenting how we can connect to Amazon's services like S3/RDS for stateful services like the database/logger/registry for now, but maybe in a future version where we feel like breaking things, this could be a solution. @kalefranz, I have checked this in detail now.

We only need to do this copy for executables which have an `RPATH` containing `$ORIGIN` on Linux. Currently this PR checks that the executable bit is set will catch everything we need it to catch as well as shell scripts too and things mistakenly set as executable.

Do you want me to port my ELF interpreting code from my `virtualenv` patch and use it to determine precisely if a file needs to be copied or not? It won't be a big speed hit I don't expect, and the saving from not copying would more than make up for it in any case (and of course it would not even get run when hardlinks can be used).

Regardless, please add this to the commit message for future reference (if for no one elses then for my own):

### Start commit message

There has been a lot of confusion regarding whether symlinks are followed for
executables and also for DSOs (dynamic shared objects) and also some confusion
about what `$ORIGIN` means. This is an attempt to clear that up. Everything was
determined from reading the source code for and debugging in `glibc 2.5`.

`-Wl,-z,origin` is irrelevant, it sets `DF_1_ORIGIN` or `DF_ORIGIN` which are
documented to mean "$ORIGIN must be handled."/"Object may use DF_ORIGIN"
respectively. Thing is, neither of those defines can be found referenced
anywhere, just one occurrence each in `glibc/elf/elf.h`. It may be that it is
important in later versions of `glibc` though.

`$ORIGIN` for the executable is resolved as `readlink("/self/proc/exe")`; It
is evaluated once per process and cached in the executable's `link_map->l_origin`.
AFAICT, this is the only time that the dynamic loader ever calls `readlink()`.

This can be seen where `expand_dynamic_string_token()` calls the `DL_DST_REQUIRED
macro. `expand_dynamic_string_token()` is called once for the exe and once for
each DSO. l_origin is a `char*` at offset `0x340` in `link_map`.
`DL_DST_REQUIRED` contains:

```
if ((l)->l_origin == NULL)					      \
  {								      \
    assert ((l)->l_name[0] == '\0');				      \
    (l)->l_origin = _dl_get_origin ();				      \
```

` _dl_get_origin()` begins:
```
const char *
_dl_get_origin (void)
{
  char linkval[PATH_MAX];
  char *result;
  int len;
  INTERNAL_SYSCALL_DECL (err);

len = INTERNAL_SYSCALL (readlink, err, 3, "/proc/self/exe", linkval,
                        sizeof (linkval));
```

Subsequent DSOs set their `l_origin` to dirname(realname) in `_dl_new_object()`.
Crucially they do *not* call `readlink()` because by the time the
`if ((l)->l_origin == NULL)` check happens, `_dl_new_object()` has already set
it.

#### End of commit message.

@kalefranz - those aren't *upgrades* - they're just the package shuffling described in #6284 

After accepting the supposed *downgrades* I then did a `conda update --all` which then exactly undid all the downgrades, reverting the packages back to the defaults channel. After doing that a further `conda update --all` says I'm actually on the latest.

So the *downgrades* are in fact just shuffling the exact same version of packages between the defaults and the conda-forge channel.

I can accept that the solver doesn't  know that the downgrades aren't actually downgrades so it's doing something sensible according to it's view of the world but in practice it means an `upgrade --all` doesn't upgrade a package when it's perfectly possible to do so.

i.e. after running the below commands:
```
conda update jupyterlab
conda update--all
```
the state of my environment is exactly the same but with `jupyterlab=0.31.2` and `jupyterlab_launcher=0.10.3`.

If the end result is a valid environment a single `conda update--all` should have chosen the plan which upgraded those two packages and changed no other packages.

There is an issue withe the treatment of the PID parameters. The "external" PID values are supposed to be passed in a list [P, I , D] -- at least that is the expectation. But after conversion the values are passed to the "internal" values and to the registers in the XL320 as D, I, P because this is the order in which the registers are listed in the motor.
If I look closely at the coefficients they are correct when the pid member is handled as a [D, I, P]. As long as you are aware of this the calls will be fine.
It's unfortunate that the member is called pid and the conversions pid_to_dxl and dxl_to_pid suggesting that that external format is PID.
I would suggest changing the way the values are converted as follows:

```
def dxl_to_pid(value, model):
    return (value[2] * 0.125,
            value[1] * 0.48828125,
            value[0] * 0.004)


def pid_to_dxl(value, model):
    truncate = lambda x: int(max(0, min(x, 254)))
    return [truncate(x * y) for x, y in zip(value, (250, 2.048, 8.0))].reverse
```

It looks like the current `__iter__` behavior was introduced in 7ae05c82a6cf35f11048a50e847b468093d69263.

Prior to 2013-05-29, the method used to yield key–value pairs, but it was deliberately changed. If there are objections to this, why weren't they raised back then?

Here's one concrete problem with the key–value idea: now that fields may be unset, programs must be able to check if a value exists or is undefined. The idiomatic way to do that is

```python
if 'my_field' in my_instance: ...
```

just like for dicts. This stops making sense if the elements produced by `iter(my_instance)` do not include `'my_field'`.

Another complication with `__iter__` specifically is that it's impossible to automatically change all occurrences of `for key in instance` to match the proposed system, given that `instance` can be any identifier. No one currently relying on `__iter__` would upgrade to v2.0.

Finally, @jmsdnns has expressed a desire to preserve a degree of model/dict interchangeability.

Given these issues, I think it's safe to say that the status quo wins by default. Key–value iteration can be provided separately for those who find it useful. (We can make sure the library itself doesn't rely on a specific variant of `__iter__`.)

If someone would still like to argue for key–value iteration, I would like to see an example of a successful precedent where `__iter__` is out of sync with `__contains__` and the other container idioms.

The response from Cisco will have a link to the next page in the header when your result set is paginated.    You will need to recursively make api calls to the next url found in the header to get the additional results.    
  

  
  

  
  
>   
> On Dec 31, 2017 at 7:03 PM,  <Roy Healy (mailto:notifications@github.com)>  wrote:
>   
>   
>   
>
> To clarify where the Cisco docs are confusing:
>  From  list messages (https://developer.ciscospark.com/endpoint-messages-get.html)
>
>   
> >   
> >
> > Limit the maximum number of messages in the response.
> >
> >   
>   
>
> But  pagination (https://developer.ciscospark.com/pagination.html)  clarifies:
>
>   
> >   
> >
> > When requesting a list of resources the max query parameter may be used to control the number of items returned per page.
> >
> >   
>   
>
> So like I said, when you use the requests module you are actually getting just the first page of results as the response. The wrapper though will also see that there are additional pages available and is capable of requesting those too. In other words, all the max argument does in the wrapper is change how many requests it'd need to make to get everything. If there are 1000 messages and you set max at 1000 the wrapper makes one request, if you set max at 500 it makes two requests.

Clarifications:

*   The behavior that deviates from default Python semantics is that objects are instantiated _without_ the `()` "call" operator.

*    It is currently the case that use of the `()` "call" operator in SDK expressions is a clear indicator that a `Resource` object is communicating with a device.   This is one benefit of the slight deviation from default behavior.    

   It _may_ be overstatement to call this behavior "un-Pythonic".

    The above example code will not succeed because the root object must be constructed in the standard Pythonic way.  (This is the exception to the previously stated rule.)

   Here's an example one-liner that _will_ work:

`tm = mgmt(IPADDRESS, USERNAME, PASSWORD).tm`

Commenting on this, I have access to RedHat (CentOS) systems, running RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 and have built several python package RPMs. I agree that `bdist_rpm` is not the right way to go. The easiest thing, in my experience, has been to use [FPM](https://github.com/jordansissel/fpm/wiki) to package the python distribution, which allows the inclusion of post-install scripts and all sorts of other conveniences.

@erikb85, I think the best option would be to have the post-install script check for an existing configuration, and if nothing is found, to install with some reasonable defaults. Ideally, this would be a bash script. From there, the Travis build [could be customized](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/customizing-the-build/#Customizing-the-Build-Step) to build the RPM using FPM when tests succeed on the master branch.

FPM is convenient also because it doesn't matter what system you're building on, as long as the requisite packages are installed. It would also make outputting as a `.deb` much easier.

What I don't know anything at all about is submitting packages for official inclusion in upstream repositories for Debian, RedHat, etc., and I don't know whether that's something we'd even want to consider. However, I'm willing to look into it if there's interest.

1. [extension](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/tutorial_xontrib.html#tutorial-extensions-xontribs)
2. [events](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/tutorial_events.html)
3. [history backend](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/tutorial_xontrib.html), plus an understanding of [xonsh history](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/tutorial_hist.html) (such as replay, [lazyjson](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/tutorial_hist.html#exciting-technical-detail-lazy-json) might be useful too)
4. [import local python modules](http://xon.sh/customization.html#import-python-modules-from-a-local-directory) `sys.path.insert(0, '')`

Todo:

- [ ] create initial xontrib
- [ ] create a xonsh history backend that feeds the relevant xonsh shell history to sacred's observers
- [ ] create sacred events that trigger xonsh events, and vice versa
- [ ] ensure [interactive is enabled](http://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/experiment.html#run-the-experiment) and experiment.run() does not block xonsh, consider [xonsh.proc](https://xonsh.github.io/xonsh-docs/docs-ahundt-sphinx-readable-theme-pypi/api/proc.html)
- [ ] let config variables be changed during run

- [ ] run [experiments in parallel](https://github.com/IDSIA/sacred/issues/108#issuecomment-324411973), possibly using the queue?

```python

$ xonsh

my_experiment.py my_dataset.py

$ ds = my_
$ import my_experiment

$ config = {'a': 3, 'b': 4}

> I like @f0k's idea of allowing this DictMergeLayer to work with both separate layers providing the different inputs, and a single layer providing a dict of inputs (or combinations of the two). Although I'm having a hard time imagining the API for this :)

Yes, exactly. It might be easier to treat this independently -- i.e., merge layers are strictly for merging multiple layers, and *every* layer may choose to return an output dictionary/list, or choose to accept an input dictionary/list from its input layer(s). IIRC, the latter wouldn't even require any changes to `get_output()`. I'll post some thoughts on this in #337, though.
The alternative would be that all layers are allowed to produce multiple outputs, but only MergeLayers are allowed to consume multiple inputs. This would enforce that every layer accepting multiple inputs could be fed either from a single multi-output layer or multiple single-output layers. That might be good as well... hmm... it turns out that the two issues are quite entangled!

> If we change `MergeLayer` to accept dicts, that would also maybe break user-created `MergeLayer` subclasses (if a dict was passed).

Yes, when forcefully merging both semantics into one base class, we'd end up with subclasses that only support one behavior or the other. Probably there aren't any use cases that would require both ways to be accepted, so it's better to split them right away.
This still leads us into another pesky name discussion. We cannot change the name of `MergeLayer` for compatibility purposes, but we could add a new alias for it, or rename it and alias the new name to `MergeLayer`.
What would be a good pair of names then? `DictMergeLayer` and `ListMergeLayer`? `NamedMergeLayer` and `OrderedMergeLayer`?

> the nature of angr is dynamic symbolic execution

ouch... It hurts so much for people working on static analysis support in angr...

Learning angr doesn't naturally tell you _how_ to find vulnerabilities. angr is a tool that assists you to analyze and understand programs, not a guide in vulnerability discovery, or a guarantee or an insurance that says "vulnerabilities must be found if you use angr". It is still up to you to learn _what_ you should do in order to find bugs in real software. And, you'll soon find out, that finding software vulnerabilities is a hard job (for the majority of people). I doubt there is any bug hunting 101 that holds your hand and show you how angr can be used in bug hunting - it's a cool idea, and we might consider that in the far future, but no one has done that up to now.

Personally I have no idea how bug hunting works. I would recommend a book, _The Bug Hunter's Diary_, as a beginner's guide to bug hunting. It's a nice book to read, and it shows many details in how a human finds bugs in real software, and what tools are useful at what time. angr is not mentioned, but you can use some approaches from the book on angr as well.

In the end, please properly case your title. All uppercase looks pretty offensive, as offensive as angr ;)

Stephan,

I think you understood right, but the area definition in pyresample refers to the outer boundary of the area. So you have to subtract/add half a pixel on the corners.

Your mycorners are:
`[-5568753.6104812799, -5565753.2044411488, 5565753.2044411488, 5568753.6104812799]`

But they should rather be:
`[-5570253.813501345, -5567253.4074612139, 5567253.4074612139, 5570253.813501345]`

So your code should looks something like this:
```
    xscale = (x_proj[-1] - x_proj[0]) / (x_proj.shape[0] - 1)
    yscale = (y_proj[-1] - y_proj[0]) / (y_proj.shape[0] - 1)
    mycorners = [x_proj[0] - xscale / 2., -y_proj[-1] - yscale /
                 2., x_proj[-1] + xscale / 2., -y_proj[0] + yscale / 2.]
```

-Adam

These changes seem to reduce the time to complete a hello world job dramatically. We're now down to 1 call to finalize a successful job with additional debug information being requested for jobs in other states. This looked to be the best compromise to getting all information on all jobs.

A revealing mock test on this branch is:
```
from Ganga.GPI import jobs
from Ganga.GPIDev.Adapters.IBackend import IBackend
from Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy import stripProxy
from Ganga.Utility.Config import setConfigOption
setConfigOption('DIRAC', 'serializeBackend', True)
for _ in range(10):
    j = jobs(424)
    j.backend.reset()
    IBackend.master_updateMonitoringInformation([stripProxy(j)])
```
(were 424 is a completed job which was submitted to the DIRAC backend)

The main amount of time on this branch is spend in the execute commands against DIRAC (of which there are only ~20 (I think) calls to DIRAC to reset and finalize the job 10 times)

This represents a significant speed improvement over the code currently in develop which took about ~10min to execute the equivalent command but now is down to about ~3min. (I suspect this isn't due to changes in system activity)

There's some internal discussion about releasing some of the SDK middleware for Linux developers (as binary releases). No final decisions on that front.

The ScenePerception module in the SDK implements a classical KinectFusion style algorithm, accelerated in OpenCL 1.2 and with custom extensions for larger spaces similar in functionality detailed in the Kintinuous paper.

PCL has a CUDA accelerated version of KinFu which works reasonably well. 

There's a few more KinFu implementations floating around on GitHub, most of the them variants on the one found in PCL. Outside of that, there's a number of state-of-the-art implementations of pose tracking + meshing that vision researchers have released in the past few years... It's a mixed bag of dependencies and licenses. InfiniTAM is probably the easiest to get up and running because someone created a librealsense wrapper, but it is for noncommercial use only. InfiniTAM optionally uses a voxel hashing backend which, if used directly, is more permissively licensed. 

https://github.com/victorprad/InfiniTAM/pull/39 (links directly to a pull request with librealsense compatibility)
https://github.com/nachtmar/VoxelHashing
https://github.com/qianyizh/ElasticReconstruction
https://github.com/mp3guy/ElasticFusion
https://github.com/mp3guy/Kintinuous
https://github.com/icg-moca/Fusion (opencl)
https://github.com/Nerei/kinfu_remake (CUDA PCL version ripped out of PCL)
https://github.com/personalrobotics/OpenChisel (needs separate pose estimation)
https://github.com/tum-vision/fastfusion (also needs pose estimation)

Hope that helps!

I'd say:

* It's not clear to me that ``featurize_many`` should natively support pd DataFrame or Series in the first place. Maybe it should be expected that the user can follow the API and just throw in "pd.values" to ``featurize.many`` rather than throwing in a DataFrame. What if there is some other matrix-like object but not quite a matrix that people use, do we need to support that too?
* If you really do think it's useful to add pd.DataFrame and pd.Series as supported inputs for featurize_many, the docstring for the function should reflect that.
* If you are adding explicit hacks intended for pd.Dataframe and pd.Series, then I guess it's fine to use isinstance() as in the original solution.
* Finally, there seems to be different ways of checking whether something is "iterable" within a few lines of code. There is the ``hasattr(entries, '__getitem__')`` and also ``isinstance(entries[0], (tuple, list, np.ndarray))``. I don't really like either solution - the first will consider a string as iterable, which is not something you want I think, and the second might not be a complete list. But other than something like "isinstance(entries, collections.Iterable) and not isinstance(entries, str)" I'm not sure what else to do. But at least it should be the same method since I think you are doing the same check.

Hello @computron ,

To answer your questions:

- The time spent checking for the oxidation states is noticeable, but not large. The featurizer where the effect of checking should be the largest is OxidationStates, which only measures statistics of the oxidation states and does not do any other calculations. The total time taken for checking there is 7% of the total time to featurize dataframe. What do you think of adding a general "debug" mode to the BaseFeaturizer class as a uniform way of turning off/on checking? This could encourage other featurizer implements to make user-friendly error messages, and not worry about the computational cost.
- I think you make a good point about not deviating from the interface. Rather than kwargs, I opted for adding the ability to change which oxidation states are retrieved as a class attribute. Otherwise, keeping track of which AbstractData classes support what kwargs could get confusing.

I look forward to your comments on this PR. Sorry for its large size.

The concern I had with **Create Project** as a classmethod of ```Project``` (if not folded into \__init \__) is that you'd have to create a project from a previously initialized project object containing its own token and url, which seems awkward at best.

What if we created a separate class to the effect of ```ProjectManager``` which would allow for creation of new projects and management of initialized ```Project``` objects? Maybe something with some of the following functionality,

| Project     | Project Title | Source      | API token  |

| Project 2      | Title 2 | url 2      |  token 2 |

        super().__init__(get_response=get_response)

        return response

Yes, one would certainly expect that Polymer should make it possible to hide unused charts rather than removing their DOM tree. But it doesn't. The problem is that Polymer has no way to say, "_this chart_ is associated with _this tag_". If the set of visible tags changes from `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` to `[2, 3, 4, 5]`, then instead of simply removing or hiding Chart 1, Polymer will say, "okay: Chart 1, load Chart 2's data; Chart 2, load Chart 3's data, …; Chart 4, load Chart 5's data; remove Chart 5 from the DOM."

The concept that we want is very simple: [give each child a key](https://facebook.github.io/react/docs/lists-and-keys.html#keys) that is used by the reconciler. The Polymer team have told me that there is no way to do this in Polymer 1.x, and that I should file a feature request for 2.x…but that won't help us, as we are not planning to migrate to 2.x.

Here's how the best practices guide justifies it:

> Tests should be straightforward enough to be 'obviously correct' upon inspection. Control structures (like loops and conditionals) can increase the chance for errors. When a test fails, having complex logic in the test makes it harder to diagnose the root cause of the failure.
>
> It's usually impossible for production code to exhaustively list every output for each possible input, so we have to use the full power of our programming languages there. But test code doesn't need to cover every input - only a small set of representative values that mark the interesting cases in the code. Increasing the complexity of a single test by using conditionals or loops to cover multiple input types can quickly lead to logic errors in the tests themselves. In other words, tests should strive to be "above suspicion" when it comes to faulty behavior, lest they require tests of their own!
>
> Tests written without control structures are clearer since the reader doesn't have to do any mental computations to understand them, and more likely to be correct since it's harder to have bugs in code without these constructs.
>
> When tests do need their own logic, such logic should often be moved out of the test bodies and into utilities and helper functions. Since such helpers can get quite complex, it's usually a good idea for any nontrivial test utility to have its own tests.
>
> Exception: It it okay to loop through an enumeration of values (e.g. an enum or a list of error codes) to perform a common set of assertions on them, often called data-driven testing. However, if you find yourself introducing conditionals to further optimize your data- driven tests (e.g. to apply different asserts to different inputs) you are better off creating separate test cases instead.

> Just one quick clarification: since you modified the way the property itself works, there is no need to touch the (global) metaclass to add a new static property to some class, right?

That's right. The metatype is no longer mutated when adding new static properties. It's only required to override the default `type.__setattr__()`. That's a one-time upfront change -- here is pretty much the entire implementation of the shared `pybind11_type` metatype:
```python
class pybind11_type(type):
    def __setattr__(cls, name, value):
        prop = cls.__dict__[name]
        if isinstance(prop, pybind11_static_property):
            pybind11_static_property.__set__(prop, cls, value)
        else:
            type.__setattr__(cls, name, value)
```

And this is only needed since `Type.prop = a` becomes `Type.__setattr__(a)` instead of the needed `Type.prop.__set__(a)`. The other direction (`a = Type.prop`) is fine and results in `a = Type.prop.__get__()`. The instance properties are also fine (`a = instance.prop` and `instance.prop = a` regularly resolve to `__get__()` and `__set__()`). It's just this one case when assigning to the `Type` itself.

> I'm wondering if there is some use in not quite deprecating py::metaclass() yet: manually specifying the metaclass to be used (which pybind11 can't yet do but which was going to be in my PR) could be useful in some contexts.

I think that shouldn't be a problem. The default `metaclass()` constructor can be deprecated while adding new (non-deprecated) `metaclass(something, ...)` constructor(s). (I'm assuming specifying a custom metaclass would require at least one parameter to be passed to the constructor.)

Following the topic I really think that **avoiding copies should be the default behavior whenever possible** because this is exactly what most people expect! I wanted to comment on some of the things that have been said here.

> We disagreed on whether --auth-hook should always be run or whether it should be specific to --manual, but concluded that this would be the prerogative of whoever implements this refactor.

I have a strong preference for making the hooks specific to the manual plugin. Doing otherwise will result in additional complexity both in the implementation and in the user experience. It is additional complexity for the implementation because the Certbot core needs to call into multiple places for both authentication and cleanup and it complicates error handling. It is additional complexity for the user as I don't think there is an intuitive answer to whether the hooks should run before or after the plugin they run with and it creates the strange situation where people are forced to choose an arbitrary plugin that does work they don't care about only to use the auth and cleanup hooks. This view is not inconsistent with what Peter wrote here, assuming I'm the one doing the work and I'll implement this as desired. I brought this up because it informs my next point.

> --script-auth and --script-cleanup could become --auth-hook and --cleanup-hook; that would require moving them out of a plugin but that's doable.

Because I think these flags should only be used for one plugin, I think it is a very bad idea to not differentiate them from the other hooks in some way. For this reason, I was planning on implementing them as `--manual-auth-hook` and `--manual-cleanup-hook`. Perhaps I should have been more vocal about my disagreement here as I definitely do not agree we should just call them `--auth-hook` and `--cleanup-hook`.

My options in order of preference are to name these flags:

1. `--<plugin>-<verb>-hook`
2. Not call them hooks at all
3. `--auth-hook` and `--cleanup-hook`

These flags are essentially hooks provided by the user, however, because I think they should only be applied to one plugin, they should be clearly differentiated in some way. I like the first option, because I think it clearly says they are hooks for that plugin. If we don't add a plugin name prefix, I do not agree that these options should be called hooks.

> The current manual plugin users have either scripted around it or have some kind of process around it. I don't think there are actual conversion factors.

Conversation factors may be minimal. The main reason this was pushed for was to reduce to the number of plugins that Certbot ships with. @jsha's comment that I agree with was that plugin choice is probably the hardest decision that Certbot users have to make. Let's not add more options unless it is necessary.

> Omitting the current manual plugin pause-to-wait-for-challenge-deployment functionality would break scripts for users that have scripted around the manual plugin altogether.

I planned on implementing this effectively like this for most methods in the plugin:
```
def foo():
    if self.conf('auth') and self.conf('cleanup'):
        script_plugin_foo()
    else:
        manual_plugin_foo()
```
The actual implementation is likely to be cleaner/cleverer, but that is how I see the behavior. Perhaps both hooks shouldn't be required, but my basic idea was to have two completely separate code paths. The pause to wait functionality would not be changed.

> I think the functionality is very beneficial for larger integrations, so I think it should be promoted on plugin level instead of "hiding" in the concept of hooks.

I agree this functionality is a big win for a lot of people given the current complexity of writing a Certbot plugin. I think these hooks are only "hidden" for existing Certbot users. Like we're experiencing for the Nginx plugin, many people don't realize that the plugin has changed. For new users, however, and for the long term, big picture of Certbot, I don't think there is a clear win to having this functionality in a separate plugin. I think we can be very clear in Certbot's help, documentation, and UI that the manual plugin is the DIY plugin, whether that be writing your own scripts or performing the task by hand.

> It would be counterintuitive to run manual (or any plugin in that effect) as a null plugin, functioning only as a container for auth-hook.

I completely agree that running a plugin as a null plugin just to get the hooks is all kinds of awkward as I outlined in my first section in this post. I'm not sure if this changes your opinion here, but as I said above I basically want to change `manual`'s behavior when an auth hook is provided. The normal functionality of `manual`, which is of minimal value with the addition of auth hooks, would not be executed when an auth hook is given on the command line.

If anyone would like to express any additional thoughts on this before I start implementing this, I would love to hear them.

I have no objections to a documentation webpage, I actually think we should definitely do this, but I prefer to have documentation in the CLI too. This is how most Unix CL programs are distributed and I am of the opinion that Scour should be a good citizen when it can be invoked as a CL program, even when written in Python. The user probably does not care about the programming language after all.

Man pages do not need to be installed in system locations, they can be installed in local environments too. Where Scour itself is installed is ultimately the choice of the user. If `pip install` installs the `scour` binary into`/usr/local/bin`, then the man page is installed in `/usr/local/share/man`. Similarly, if `pip install --user` installs the binary into `~/.local/bin`, then the man page goes into `~/.local/share/man`. Even installing it within `virtualenv` is supported without problems. `pip` supports the installation of additional files and handles the installation _and_ removal. In my own tests, all that was required was adding `data_files=[('scour.1', ['share/man/man1/scour.1'])]` to `setup.py`. `pip` then determines the top-level directory upon installation, just as it does for the binary file. It is even possible to override the data path with `pip --install-option`. `man` itself searches for man pages automatically if the `$PATH` is set correctly.

As Ede123 said, Sphinx can handle both at the same time. I think Scour would benefit from having a fleshed-out man page. I myself tested the man-page functionality with a Ruby program called [RONN](http://rtomayko.github.io/ronn/), which takes a markdown file and produces a correctly-formatted man page as well as an HTML page of it. It is an alternative to think about to the more complex Sphinx.

Ok, I devised a test case that shows that we cannot arbitrarily remove "m0 0".

The original:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg width="600" height="480" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <path d="m150 240h200m0 0 150 150v-300z" fill="none" stroke="#000"/>
</svg>
```

The scour'ed version:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg width="600" height="480" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
 <path d="m150 240h200l150 150v-300z" fill="none" stroke="#000"/>
</svg>
```

The difference difference in the xml is that a "m0 0 150 150" is replaced by "l150 150". The original draws a line and a triangle while the scour'ed version draws a deformed rectangle. From this I suspect that it is unsafe to rewrite "m0 0" if the subpath ends with a z/Z command. I.e.

 * `... m0 0 150 150 ... z` is unsafe
 * `... m0 0 150 150 ... mX Y ... z` _might_ be rewritable to `l150 150 ... mX Y ... z`

My primary concern here is one of compat.  If we move to TS3 and then use something from TS3 that isn't supported on previous versions, that puts on a burden on customers to have to upgrade.  So, my personal preference would be that we upgrade the version of TS we use if/when there's a feature in it we need which warrants it.  

In other words, we are more compatible with more users, the lower the version of TS that we use.  The 2 line itself has huge adoption, and It's unclear to me what we're necessarily buying by moving away from it.
