The suggested items based on keepachangelog.com versus what we have here: https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/releases:

* It’s made for humans, not machines, so legibility is crucial. - We already do this when we note the major features of the release so non-technical users can easily review them. 
* Easy to link to any section (hence Markdown over plain text). - We don't currently do this, and the changelog isn't usually such a long list that we currently need this
* One sub-section per version. - We fulfill this though not perfectly, each release has a different area on the page (though this could be condensed into a version specific changelog that has multiple releases).
* List releases in reverse-chronological order (newest on top). - We already do this.
* Write all dates in YYYY-MM-DD format. (Example: 2012-06-02 for June 2nd, 2012.) It’s international, sensible, and language-independent. - We do this automatically via the GitHub release details noting when the build was released, though the formatting isn't perfect.
* Explicitly mention whether the project follows Semantic Versioning. - We already follow semantic versioning, I don't really feel like this needs to be explicitly noted, those who don't follow semantic versioning should probably note this instead. ;)
* Each version should:
  * List its release date in the above format. - As noted above we kind of do this.
  * Group changes to describe their impact on the project, as follows: - Currently we simply group all of these together as we don't have enough changes to really justify categories. If we did that we would have more categories than changes in many cases.
    * Added for new features.
    * Changed for changes in existing functionality.
    * Deprecated for once-stable features removed in upcoming releases.
    * Removed for deprecated features removed in this release.
    * Fixed for any bug fixes.
    * Security to invite users to upgrade in case of vulnerabilities.

Overall there are definitely a few changes we could implement to provide a better changelog. I would actually say we should leave the existing changelog as is for the releases, and create a new user centric changelog for the docs specifically (though that does take some visibility away from contributors in the event people wish to donate to them or thank them in some way).

> Regarding why I want to run them in the same thread, that's because a call may block and I don't want devices to block each other. I.e. I don't mind a device blocking other calls to the same device, but I don't want that to leak between devices. Similarly, I don't want to have more than one thread per device because I'd rather multiple calls to the same device line up than spawn more threads due to the dubious multi-threaded safety of the underlying devices.

It sounds like using a `trio.Lock` for each device might be a simpler way to accomplish this?

> [2x speedup for your class over `trio.run_in_worker_thread`]

That's pretty interesting. What platform did you use to measure this? Windows?

> I wonder if there are downsides of this compared to how you did `run_in_worker_thread` and why you didn't do it like I did (delay cancel and raise it in the task later)?

Just because if the operation has already completed successfully, then it wasn't cancelled :-). As much as possible for the low-level trio operations I try to maintain the rule that cancelled means that the operation had no effect. Like if a timeout expires but before you can react to the timeout, the operation completes successfully... why throw away that work and pretend it failed? it's already done :-).

> The reason why I think this executor example would be useful is not only my use case that I described above, but with some modifications you can use it to schedule an event in an existing event loop framework (e.g. when you're using some gui framework that provides one) and have a trio task proxy it fully. This is really what I wanted; a high level bullet proof way of proxying an event scheduled in another even loop with a trio task

Right, this is definitely interesting! It does require some case-by-case work of course though. You don't actually want to use a queue to communicate between trio and kivy, for example, because you don't want to do blocking queue operations in either thread, plus they both have a solid set of non-blocking tools to work with. Instead you should use `call_soon` as the low-level primitive for sending stuff from the kivy thread to trio, and it [looks like](https://github.com/kivy/kivy/wiki/Working-with-Python-threads-inside-a-Kivy-application) `kivy.clock.Clock.schedule_once()` as the low-level primitive for sending stuff from trio to kivy, and then build `run_in_kivy_thread` on top of those. (I don't know enough about kivy's idioms to know what would be an idiomatic API for `run_in_trio_thread_from_kivy`. It sounds like you're mostly interested in the other direction anyway.) And this kind of problem in building fundamental plumbing is exactly why `yield_indefinitely` and friends are exposed in `trio.hazmat`.

Regarding your comments on docs and examples: the feedback is definitely appreciated. I may not be able to do too much about it in the immediate future because of limited hours in a day and because I'm still trying to focus on getting trio feature-complete :-). But it's useful to hear in any case. (And if you have more specific suggestions or get an itch and decide to submit some edits as a PR, that'd be very welcome too.)

Hope that helps!

Here is an alternative proposal that I think could be considered consistent:

Top level include/ directory contains

GalSim.h
galsim/

All other GalSim header files are in the galsim subdirectory.  The user would typically just include GalSim.h to get access to everything that is in the libgalsim library.

Within the galsim subdirectory, we have one header file for each sub-package (sbp, hsm, etc.) which would include all other necessary header files.  So if you only want to load a single package, rather than all of galsim, you would for write, e.g.

#include "galsim/SBProfile.h"

which would include everything that Python imports as 

import galsim.sbprofile

Everything that is in the SBProfile package would be in the sbp (or sbprofile if preferred) namespace.  Not galsim::sbp.

And I would add that within our own code, we always use the package-specific header file rather than GalSim.h as a way of pointing out which namespace things are coming from.  The namespace would always be consistent with which header file you needed to include to gain access to an object or function.
